summary: O'Neil reflects on her experience launching a data journalism program at Columbia, identifying fundamental cultural clashes between computer science and journalism that make the field difficult to teach. Computer science emphasizes efficiency, narrow problem-solving, and getting code to work fast, while journalism prioritizes broad context-gathering, understanding ambiguity, and synthesizing information into compelling narratives. She argues that data journalism requires a middle ground that combines programming power with deep awareness of human bias in data collection. The piece concludes that success depends not just on academic programs but on media organizations actually valuing and hiring thoughtfully trained data journalists.
key_points: Academia's departmental silos create unintentional hostility toward interdisciplinary fields like data journalism
Computer science training rewards narrow, efficient problem-solving without questioning context or design choices
Journalism training emphasizes breadth, context, and awareness of knowledge gaps before drawing conclusions
Computer scientists can be 'blithe with respect to messy human details' and miss selection bias because their programs technically work
The ideal data journalism training would combine programming power with deep investigation of human bias in data
money_quotes: In cartoonish form, introductory computer science classes are competitive weeder classes that promote a certain kind of narrow, clever, problem-solving approach.
It's kind of the least context-laden and most content-heavy way of learning that you can imagine.
The context is what makes a topic interesting and important and newsworthy.
Journalists are ever aware of the things they know they don't know, and desperately want to fill in their knowledge gaps because otherwise they feel fraudulent.
Computer scientists don't care about not knowing things as long as their programs work.
They can be blithe with respect to messy human details, which of course means they sometimes don't notice or figure out their data has selection bias because they got an answer.
Both sides emphasize a kind of learning and a definition of success that the other side is blind to.
The outside world has to actually want to hire the emerging thoughtful fruit of that labor.
key_insight: Data journalism struggles because computer science rewards efficiency without context while journalism rewards context without efficiency, and bridging this gap requires both academic investment and industry demand for thoughtfully trained practitioners.
